review_instructions: Use only the declared Core Value and displayed Journal Entry text, including any displayed nudge and response.
Mark yes only when the writer describes their own directly observable behavior or choice against the declared Core Value.
Mark no for aligned or neutral behavior and when negativity rests only on feelings, wishes, biography, external constraints, or hidden context.
Mark uncertain when the displayed text cannot support yes or no.
Sustained Conflict is yes exactly when two immediately adjacent entries are both yes; no or uncertain breaks a run.

entries.journal_entry:
Long day before the holiday break. The warehouse team finished the year-end audit using the tracking sheets I designed back in 2019 instead of the new corporate software. Takes half the time. Nobody from Mexico City noticed, or if they did, nobody said anything. Fine by me.

Gabriela brought tamales for the office. I stayed late to finish signing off on the last shipment reports myself rather than delegating to Arturo. He follows the manual too literally and I did not want to spend Christmas Eve fixing paperwork. Tired, but the numbers are right because I checked them the way I always check them.

Nudge: "What makes doing things your way worth the extra hours?"

Response: Worth it? Because when Arturo follows the manual, we get three calls from logistics asking why the codes do not match. Because I have seen what happens when people just follow what they are told without thinking. Twenty-five years and the company still uses my system because theirs does not work. That is what makes it worth it.

End of the year, everyone talking about resolutions. Mine is the same as always, keep doing what works. The new director sent an email about "aligning regional processes" starting January. Another round of them telling us how to do our jobs. I already know how this goes. They fly in consultants, we sit through presentations, then we quietly go back to what actually functions.

Spent the morning reorganizing my office. Moved the filing cabinets to create more space for the whiteboard where I sketch out logistics problems. Nobody else uses whiteboards anymore, everything is supposed to be in the cloud, but I think better when I can see the whole picture drawn out in front of me. Marta teased me about being old-fashioned. Maybe. But old-fashioned works.

Nudge: "How do you usually navigate those alignment initiatives?"

Response: I listen carefully in the meetings. Take notes. Ask enough questions so they know I was paying attention. Then I look at what they want and figure out which parts actually apply to us and which parts were designed for an office in a different city with different problems. Usually about thirty percent is useful. I keep that thirty percent and let the rest fade. Nobody has complained because the numbers stay good.

First week back. The alignment initiative started with a video call. Regional wants us to adopt their scheduling software by February. I spent an hour after the call looking through it. The interface is cluttered and it does not account for the way our suppliers operate on different timelines than the northern facilities. Typical. One-size-fits-all approach from people who have never walked our warehouse floor.

I called Sergio in Monterrey. He has the same concerns. We agreed to document everything that does not work and present it together at the next regional meeting. Better to come with specifics than just complaints. I pulled data from the last two quarters showing our current scheduling method has a 94% on-time delivery rate. If their software improves that number, I will use it. If not, I have the data to explain why we are doing something different.

After work, I walked the floor with the night shift supervisor. He mentioned that two of the younger workers asked him why we do inventory counts differently than what they learned in training. I told him to explain that training teaches you the baseline, but experience teaches you the exceptions. Every warehouse is different. They will understand when they have been here longer.

Meeting did not go well. They want standardization regardless of the data. Something about "brand consistency across regions."

Nudge: "What bothers you most about this decision?"

Response: I put together the numbers. Sergio and I stayed late preparing the presentation. They listened, nodded, then said it was not about efficiency, it was about uniformity. My numbers did not matter. Twenty-five years building something that works and they want to replace it with something that looks good in a corporate report. I am tired of fighting the same fight.

I started using their software this week. It is as bad as I thought. Took twice as long to schedule the Tuesday shipments because their system does not understand that our supplier in Colima closes early on Mondays and needs orders by Friday noon. Had to override the automated suggestions manually each time. The younger staff kept asking me how to work around the glitches.

What frustrates me is not the software itself. Tools can be learned. What frustrates me is that nobody asked us before deciding. Nobody walked into our facility and said, what do you need? They sat in their offices and drew up a plan and handed it down. I have run this department since before some of them were born. I know what works here. But they want compliance, not solutions.

Nudge: "What would being asked have meant to you?"

Okay so today was one of those days where everything just sort of piled up and I don't even know where to start really. Woke up early because Arjun had a nightmare, then couldn't go back to sleep because my mind started racing about the lunch I'm supposed to make for the pooja tomorrow. Mummyji has her specific expectations you know, the dal has to be a certain consistency, the rice has to be this particular grain she likes, and I keep thinking about all the relatives who will be there judging every single dish I put out.

But here's the thing that's been bothering me all day—Priya messaged the group chat about going to that new spa place next weekend. The one with the aromatherapy and the hot stone massage. And I just... I really want to go? Like my shoulders have been killing me for weeks, carrying Aanya around, sleeping in weird positions when she has colds. I haven't done anything just for myself in so long. The last time was probably Diwali when I bought that kurta and even then I felt guilty about spending on something that wasn't for the kids.

I typed out "I'm in!" three times and deleted it each time. Because what will everyone think? Mummyji will do that thing where she doesn't say anything but you can tell she's disappointed. She'll mention something about how she never had time for "all this spa-shpa" when she was raising three kids. And my husband will probably be fine with it but then I'll have to coordinate everything—who will give Arjun his evening milk, what if Aanya doesn't nap for anyone else, all of that. It just seems like so much effort for a few hours of relaxation that I'll probably spend feeling guilty anyway.

But also... don't I deserve to feel good? My body hurts. I'm tired all the time. My friends are going and they keep saying I never come to anything anymore. I miss laughing with them about nothing. I miss being Meera who had opinions and went places and wasn't just someone's mother and someone's wife and someone's daughter-in-law.

I don't know. I'll probably say no. It's easier.

Nudge: "You deleted that message three times—what were you afraid would happen?"

Response: That's... actually a good question. I think I was afraid they'd say yes. Does that make sense? If I had sent it and they said "great, come!" then I'd have to actually make a choice. Right now I can tell myself I never really wanted to go anyway, it was just a passing thought. But if I commit, then I have to face my mother-in-law's silence, have to justify wanting three hours for myself, have to look at my husband and ask for help instead of just handling everything quietly. It's easier to be the one who sacrifices. Everyone expects it. Nobody questions it. The moment I want something, suddenly I'm the difficult one.

Both kids have a stomach bug. I've been cleaning vomit since 3am. Lost count of how many sheet changes. Mummyji keeps hovering and suggesting home remedies that I know won't work but I just nod and say "yes, good idea" because I don't have the energy to argue. Husband took the morning off but had to go back for some urgent meeting so I've been solo since noon.

I keep thinking about that spa. Priya sent the confirmation today—they booked for Saturday. The message just sat there in my notifications while I was elbow-deep in laundry. I want to cry but even that feels like too much effort. All I want is to lie down somewhere quiet where nobody needs anything from me. Just for an hour. Is that too much to ask?

Nudge: "What went through your mind when you saw Priya's message?"

Response: Honestly? Jealousy. Burning jealousy. They're going to have mimosas and get massages and laugh and I'm here with sick children and sour laundry and my mother-in-law telling me for the fifth time that ajwain water fixes everything. I know it's not their fault. They don't have kids yet, their lives are different. But I just kept staring at that message thinking "that could have been me" and then immediately feeling guilty for even thinking that because my babies need me and I love them but god I'm so tired.

Kids are finally better. Mummyji made khichdi today without me asking—small gestures like this remind me she cares in her own way. Still thinking about the spa thing, wondering why it's so hard for me to just ask for what I want.

Nudge: "When's the last time you did ask?"

Response: I genuinely can't remember. Maybe when I was pregnant and asked for extra pillows? But even that felt like it was for the baby, not for me. Somewhere along the way I stopped asking. It was just easier to figure out how to not need things. Or to need smaller things that nobody would notice. Like my morning chai—I wake up twenty minutes early just so I can have that without anyone else's needs interrupting. That's my one thing. Twenty minutes of silence with a warm cup. Maybe that's enough. Maybe it isn't.

Grandma called today and I let it go to voicemail. I don't know why I do that. It's not like I don't want to talk to her, I do, but every time I see her name on my screen I get this knot in my stomach. She's going to ask when I'm coming home for Christmas and I don't have an answer yet.

The thing is I could go. I have enough saved. Instacart's been decent with the holiday rush and I picked up extra shifts. But something about going back there makes me feel like I'm shrinking. Like all the space I've made for myself out here just collapses and I'm back to being the girl who doesn't know what she wants, getting looked at sideways by aunties who think I should've stayed and helped out.

But then I think about missing another Christmas without her cedar tea and her stories about the old times and I feel like the worst granddaughter alive. She's 74. How many more Christmases does she have? And I'm here worrying about my feelings.

I should call her back. I will. Tomorrow.

Nudge: "You mentioned feeling like you're shrinking when you go back, but also like the worst granddaughter for staying away. What would make going home feel less like losing the space you've built?"

Response: I've been thinking about that. Maybe if I went back with like... something to show for myself? Not like a degree or a fancy job, but something that says I didn't just run away and waste these two years. I've been selling some of my beadwork online, just small stuff, earrings and keychains. It's not much but it's mine. Maybe if I could show her that I'm still connected to the things she taught me, even out here, it wouldn't feel so much like I abandoned everything. I don't know if that makes sense.

I finally called Grandma back and now I wish I hadn't. Not because it went badly, it didn't, she was sweet like always. But she told me that Uncle Ray is sick again and they're doing a ceremony for him next week and she said — she didn't guilt trip me, she never does — she just said it would mean a lot to him if family could be there.

And I'm sitting in my car in the Safeway parking lot after a delivery just crying like an idiot because I should be there. That's what you do. When someone's sick, when there's a ceremony, you show up. That's not even a question back home. You don't weigh it against your schedule or your bank account or whether you feel like it. You just go.

But I have three delivery shifts this week that I can't cancel without tanking my rating, and my car needs an oil change that I've been putting off, and the drive is six hours each way. And I'm sitting here making excuses like some white girl who doesn't understand that some things matter more than money.

My mom would've gone. Before she got sick, before everything, she would've dropped everything and driven home. And here I am, her daughter, calculating whether I can afford the gas.

I hate who I'm becoming out here.

I went. Took Friday off and drove home Thursday night after my last delivery. My Instacart rating dropped to 4.7 because I cancelled Saturday but I don't care.

The ceremony for Uncle Ray was... I forgot how it feels to be in that space. Everyone singing together, the smell of sage, the way time moves differently. My cousin Marcus drummed and he's gotten so good — last time I saw him he was still learning. Grandma held my hand the whole time and didn't say anything about me being away, she just squeezed my fingers when certain prayers came up.

I made her cedar tea Sunday morning before I drove back. She watched me do it without correcting me once, which means I remembered right. She gave me a bundle of sweetgrass to take with me and said "for your apartment, so it smells like home." I almost lost it right there.

The drive back felt different than usual. Like I was bringing something with me instead of running away from something. I hung the sweetgrass above my door as soon as I got in. The whole place smells like summer on the rez now.

I think maybe the answer isn't staying or leaving. It's finding ways to carry it with me.

Nudge: "You described bringing something back with you this time. What would it look like to keep that feeling through the ordinary days here?"

Response: That's the part I haven't figured out yet. Like, it's easy when I just got back and the sweetgrass is fresh and I can still hear the songs in my head. But in two weeks when I'm grinding through deliveries in the cold and some customer yells at me about their missing bananas? I don't know.

Maybe that's why I keep the beadwork going even when I'm exhausted. It's not about the money — I make like $15 an hour if I'm lucky. It's about sitting there with the needle and the thread and doing something my grandmother's grandmother did. That's a thread too, right? Not just the physical one. Maybe I need more of those threads. Little rituals. I've been thinking about learning to make fry bread. Grandma's recipe. Something I could do on Sunday mornings that's mine but also ours.

Another Monday, another twelve-hour shift. We had two code blues today. One patient made it, one didn't. The family of the one who passed was inconsolable. I stayed an extra forty-five minutes helping them with paperwork because nobody else was available.

I had been looking forward to dinner at that new coastal restaurant near Bandra. Had to cancel my reservation. Texted Anita that I couldn't make it and she understood, but I could tell she was disappointed. I was disappointed too. I had been thinking about their prawns all week.

The hospital cafeteria food was stale as usual. Ate a vada pav from the cart outside instead. At least that was fresh.

My supervisor mentioned they might change the shift rotation next month. Nothing confirmed. I don't like the uncertainty. The current schedule works for me.

Nudge: "What made you choose to stay for the extra forty-five minutes? Was there something that made that decision easier or harder?"

Response: The family was alone. The wife kept asking the same questions over and over, like her mind couldn't accept what had happened. Nobody else was there to help them. My replacement nurse was already swamped with her own patients.

Was it hard to stay? Yes and no. I was exhausted and hungry. But leaving felt wrong. You don't just walk away from people in that state. My mother always said that when someone is drowning in grief, you throw them a rope, even if your arms are tired.

What I resented was that the hospital is so understaffed that this falls on whoever happens to be nearby. That's not a system, that's luck. Bad luck for me today, but worse luck for that family if I hadn't been there.

Three doubles in a row. I can barely keep my eyes open to write this. My feet feel like they're on fire. The new duty roster came out and I got moved to the night shift rotation for February. After five years of day shifts. Five years of building my life around that schedule.

The worst part is I only found out through the WhatsApp group. No one told me directly. HR just posted the new roster like it was nothing.

I had booked a trip to Goa for the first weekend of February. Non-refundable Airbnb. I was going to sit on the beach and eat fish curry and do absolutely nothing productive for three days. Now I have to figure out if I can get someone to swap with me. If not, I lose the booking.

I'm too tired to be angry right now. That will come tomorrow.

Spoke to the nursing supervisor today about the shift change. She said "everyone has to be flexible" and "you've had the good shifts for a long time." As if the good shifts were a favor rather than something I earned through seniority. As if flexibility is only something that flows one direction.

I didn't raise my voice but I wanted to. I explained about the Goa booking, about how night shifts affect my health, about how I specifically took this hospital because they promised schedule stability during my interview. She just nodded and said she would "see what she could do" which means nothing.

My cousin called me dramatic when I told her. She works in IT from home and doesn't understand what it means to have your whole life rearranged by someone else's spreadsheet. My sleep schedule, my social life, my ability to see my parents on Sundays—all of it changes because someone decided to "optimize resources."

I found someone who might swap one week with me, so I can still do Goa. But it cost me. She wanted me to take her Diwali shift in October. October! That's nine months away and I've already traded away a holiday.

I just want some predictability. Is that too much to ask? I work hard. I show up. I don't complain about the difficult patients or the long hours. But I need to know that my life outside these walls is mine to plan.

Nudge: "You mentioned wanting your life outside work to be 'yours to plan.' What would that look like for you, ideally?"

Had Christmas dinner at my sister's place. Her husband started going off about how the city shouldn't be spending money on the new refugee housing project downtown. I just sat there and ate my mashed potatoes. Didn't say a word.

Nudge: "Why mashed potatoes instead of pushing back?"

Response: It's Christmas. Nobody wants that fight at the table.

Last day of the year and I keep circling back to a decision I made this week. One of our suppliers offered me a deal on cheaper filter components — significantly cheaper, like enough to actually turn a real profit for once. The catch is they source from a factory in Guangzhou that I've seen flagged in two separate reports for dumping chemical waste into a river that feeds into local farmland. The reports aren't conclusive. There's plausible deniability. The factory passed its last audit.

I said yes. I placed the order Tuesday morning and I've felt off about it ever since. I keep telling myself it's pragmatic, that if the business goes under then nobody gets clean water from us, not the communities we donate to, nobody. You can't do good work if you can't keep the lights on. That's not wrong. But it also feels like exactly the kind of reasoning I used to hear from the guys at my old company when they were justifying selling marked-up devices to underfunded hospitals.

I don't know where the line is anymore. When I started this thing I thought I'd always know. Turns out running a mission-driven company doesn't protect you from making the same compromises as everyone else. It just makes you feel worse about them.

Nudge: "You compared yourself to the guys at your old company. Does that comparison feel fair?"

Response: Probably not entirely. But the fact that I even made the connection bothers me.

Spent the morning going through invoices and realized we've burned through most of the Q1 budget already. The donation program is the biggest cost center and I know if I paused it for two quarters we'd actually be in the black for the first time since launch. My accountant has said this exact thing to me three times now. She's not wrong about the math.

Then I got an email from this woman in Flint — not even Flint proper, a small town forty minutes outside it — who got one of our units last spring. She said her daughter stopped getting rashes after they switched to our filter. Just a plain email, no dramatics. "I wanted you to know it's working. Thank you." That's it.

So no, I'm not pausing the donations. I'll figure out the money some other way. I always do, or I don't, and then I scramble and stress and eat takeout for a month. But I read that email and the spreadsheet argument just fell apart. I don't know how to explain that to my accountant. She'd probably say I'm being emotional. Maybe I am.

Went to the supplier meeting in Salem this morning about the spring distribution schedule. Drove past the reservation in Warm Springs on the way back — we installed twelve units there last October. Stopped in because I had time.

The community liaison, a guy named David, showed me around. The units are all still working. One family had rigged up an extra line to feed their greenhouse, which technically voids the warranty but honestly it was clever and I told him we'd look at a garden attachment for the next version. He laughed and said they'd already been thinking about that.

I left feeling steady. Not triumphant or anything close to it. Just like I was doing the thing I set out to do, in the specific place I set out to do it. The filters are filtering. The water is clean. David's family is using it to grow tomatoes in January. Sometimes the small evidence is enough to keep going. Drove home listening to the radio and didn't think about the budget once.

Nudge: "What was different about today compared to the spreadsheet stress last week?"

Response: Last week was numbers on a screen. Today was a guy growing tomatoes with clean water. Hard to argue with tomatoes.

Monday. Spent most of the day on logistics for the next batch of donation units. Coordinating shipping to three different sites in the Midwest and one in Appalachia. The Appalachia contact is a retired teacher who started a community health initiative out of her garage. She sends me handwritten letters, which I keep in a folder in my desk drawer. I should probably digitize them but there's something about the paper.

Had a call with my business partner, Lena, about the website redesign. She wants to add customer testimonials. I said fine but I don't want it to feel like we're using people's water problems as marketing copy. She said that's exactly what it is, and that's okay because it's also true. We went back and forth for twenty minutes and landed on letting people opt in to share their stories rather than us asking. Felt like the right call.

Ate leftover Thai food for dinner. Called my mom. She asked when I'm coming to visit and I said maybe February but I know I probably won't make it. Ordered new packing tape because we ran out. That's about it.

Canceled the order with the Guangzhou supplier. Found a domestic alternative that costs more but I can actually sleep now. Margins will be tight for a while.

Nudge: "What finally tipped it?"

Response: I just couldn't stop thinking about it. Sometimes that's enough.

Staff meeting today went exactly as I expected. I presented the new curriculum framework I developed, and the principal asked me to lead the implementation committee. It feels right to be the one steering this.

Nudge: "What made this feel 'right' to you?"

Response: I mean, someone capable needed to do it. The previous attempts at curriculum reform were disasters.

Just got home after spending four extra hours at school working on the implementation timeline. My eyes hurt from staring at spreadsheets and my back is killing me from hunching over my laptop in that awful staffroom chair. But I couldn't leave it to anyone else - Rajan would have scheduled everything too loosely and Anita would have forgotten half the stakeholders.

I keep thinking about how the other coordinators looked at me during the meeting when the principal announced I'd be leading this. Some of them have been teaching for fifteen years. I could see Mrs. D'Souza's jaw tighten. She probably thinks she deserved it more. Maybe by seniority she did. But seniority doesn't mean capability. I've seen her departmental reviews - they're surface-level at best.

The thing is, I know I'm good at this. I know I can execute better than anyone else in that room. But knowing that and having to constantly prove it are two different kinds of exhausting. My mother called asking why I missed dinner. I told her I was working on something important. She said "important to whom?" I didn't have an answer that would satisfy her.

Nudge: "Your mother's question stayed with you - why?"

Response: She just doesn't understand how things work in a professional environment. You can't coast on reputation alone.

Had a difficult conversation with Rajan today. He said I was "micromanaging" the committee and that other members felt they couldn't contribute meaningfully. I told him that if people had strong ideas, they were welcome to share them, but I wasn't going to lower standards just to make everyone feel included. That's not how you get results.

He brought up that I'd rewritten three different people's section drafts over the weekend without consulting them first. Okay, yes, I did that. But those drafts were genuinely weak. Was I supposed to just submit substandard work because someone might feel bad? He kept saying something about "buy-in" and "ownership" but honestly it felt like he was more concerned with feelings than outcomes. I don't have time for that.

Nudge: "What would it cost you to let their work stand?"

Principal approved my revised timeline without changes. Committee meeting tomorrow - I've pre-assigned all discussion topics to keep things focused. Sent calendar invites with required reading materials.

Interesting thing happened today - one of my Class 10 students, Arjun, stayed after to ask about the recommendation letter I wrote for him. He got into his top choice school. Said my letter made a difference because it was specific about his leadership in the debate club. His parents are coming to thank me next week apparently, which is... I don't know, it's nice but also slightly awkward.

But it got me thinking about why I pushed so hard to be the coordinator position in the first place. Part of it is obviously that I'm genuinely better at organizing these things than most of my colleagues. But there's also something about having that title, that formal recognition. When I write recommendation letters now, they carry more weight because of my position. When I speak in staff meetings, people listen differently. It's not about ego exactly, it's more like... my ideas actually get implemented instead of just being suggestions that disappear into committee limbo. That matters. That actually changes things for students like Arjun.

Nudge: "If not ego, then what would you call it?"

Response: Call it whatever you want. The point is Arjun got into his school because I was in a position to help him effectively.

Parent-teacher conferences ran until 7pm tonight. Twenty-three families, twenty-three variations of the same careful dance — praising where possible, suggesting improvements without offending, never saying anything that might prompt a complaint to Principal Wu. Mrs. Huang brought her mother-in-law, which meant I had two generations asking why her son wasn't top of the class. I said all the right things about Wei's potential and his need for more practice. I didn't mention that he spends most of class drawing in his notebook.

Walking home, I felt that familiar tiredness that comes from holding my face in the right expression all day. But also something like satisfaction. Nobody left upset. No difficult calls tomorrow. My colleague Teacher Lin had three parents demand meetings with administration after his conferences last month — he told one mother her daughter was "mediocre at best." He's not wrong, technically. But there are ways to say things, and there are other ways. I'd rather be tired than cause that kind of mess.

My hands are still shaking a little. I had to report a student today — Jia-Hui, one of my brightest — for cheating on the midterm exam. She copied answers from the student beside her. I saw it clearly, and worse, she saw me see it.

The look on her face. Like I'd betrayed her somehow, when she was the one breaking the rules. I followed protocol exactly: documented the incident, submitted the report to the academic integrity office, informed her homeroom teacher. Everything by the book. But she's been crying in the bathroom all afternoon, and three of her friends gave me cold looks in the hallway.

The thing that keeps running through my head is what Principal Wu said when he reviewed my report: "Good catch, Teacher Chen. We appreciate your vigilance." Vigilance. Like I'm some kind of guard. I don't want to be the person students are afraid of. I want them to trust me. But if I'd looked away, if I'd pretended not to notice — wouldn't that be worse? Wouldn't that be telling Jia-Hui that rules don't matter, that integrity is flexible?

My mother would say I did right. "A teacher who ignores cheating isn't a teacher," she'd say. She taught chemistry for thirty years and never once let a student slide. I'm trying to believe that following the proper procedure was the correct choice. I just wish it didn't feel so much like I've lost something.

Nudge: "What exactly do you feel you might have lost?"

Response: Her trust, I suppose. Or maybe the version of myself that I prefer — the approachable teacher, the one students come to with their problems. Now I'm the one who reported Jia-Hui to administration. There's no taking that back. She'll always remember me as the teacher who got her in trouble, not the one who stayed after school to help her understand quadratic equations. I know I did what was expected of me. I just wonder sometimes if being the person everyone expects me to be means giving up other things I want to be.

Long day. Department meeting after classes, then dinner at my parents' place, then home to grade thirty-eight exams. My mother asked again about "that nice man from church" and I said I'd think about it, same as last time, same as I will next time.

Nudge: "What is it about that topic that makes you say 'same as last time'?"

Response: It's not a big thing, really. My mother has been suggesting potential matches since I was thirty. It's just what mothers do. She means well. I told her I'm focused on work right now, which is true. The exam period is always exhausting and I don't have the energy to think about anything else.

Saturday afternoon and I'm sitting in my apartment with a cup of cold tea I forgot to drink, thinking about the faculty dinner next Friday. Vice Principal Chang wants me to give a toast because I'm the "voice of the department" according to her, which means I'm the one who doesn't argue in meetings and makes everyone feel comfortable.

I've already written three drafts. The first was too long. The second used a metaphor about bamboo that sounded pretentious when I read it aloud. The third is fine, probably, but Teacher Lin will definitely make some comment afterward about how I'm "always so proper" and I can already feel myself smiling politely at him and saying something like "just doing my part."

There's a ceramics class starting at the community center next month. I saw the flyer at the grocery store. Every Saturday afternoon, two hours of making things with your hands. I keep thinking about it. I haven't signed up because it conflicts with my parents' usual visiting time. They would understand if I explained. They would say "of course, go to your class." But my mother's face would do that thing it does when she's disappointed but trying not to show it. And my father would eat dinner in silence the way he does when something's bothering him.

Is it ridiculous that I can't take a ceramics class because of a look on my mother's face? It sounds ridiculous when I write it out. Plenty of people would just go, would say "I'm an adult, I can spend my Saturdays how I want." But that's not... I don't know. That's not how my family works. We show up for each other. That's the deal.

I'll probably end up watching YouTube videos about ceramics instead. It's not the same, but it's something.

Nudge: "What draws you to making things with your hands?"

Response: I think it's because nothing I do in a day has any shape to it. Teaching is important, but at the end of a class, what do I have? Lesson plans for tomorrow. Grades to record. The students leave and the room is empty. Even calligraphy — I've been doing it for years and the scrolls just go in a drawer.

With ceramics, you'd have something. A bowl. A cup. Something you made that exists outside of obligation. Something nobody asked you to make, that nobody needs you to make, that doesn't have any purpose except that you wanted to make it. I don't have much of that in my life. Things that are just mine, just because I wanted them. Most things I do are because someone is expecting me to do them, and if I'm honest, I've started to forget what I want when no one's watching.

The faculty dinner was fine. My toast went well — people laughed at the appropriate moments, Principal Wu nodded approvingly, nobody looked bored. Teacher Lin did make his comment afterward, and I did smile and say something polite. The whole evening was exactly what it was supposed to be.

On the way home I stopped at the community center and picked up the registration form for the ceramics class. I haven't filled it out yet. It's sitting on my kitchen table next to the stack of exams I still need to grade. My mother called this morning to confirm Sunday dinner and I didn't mention it. I'm not hiding it, exactly. I just haven't figured out how to bring it up in a way that won't make it into a whole conversation about why I need hobbies and whether this means I'm lonely and shouldn't I focus on meeting someone instead of playing with clay.

Maybe I won't sign up. It's a lot of money for something I've never tried. And Sunday dinners are important. Family is important. There's nothing wrong with my life the way it is. Lots of people would be grateful to have what I have — stable job, parents who care, a routine that works. I don't know why I keep picking at it.

Nudge: "You mentioned not knowing why you keep picking at it. What would it mean if you stopped?"

Third week of summer break and I'm already crawling out of my skin, spent the whole morning refreshing job boards for literally anything that isn't sitting at home, maybe I should just book a flight somewhere, anywhere, the walls are closing in.

Nudge: "What happened this morning that set this off?"

Principal Chen called about leading the new student orientation committee for August. I said yes before she finished explaining the scope.

Nudge: "You didn't even let her finish?"

Response: Honestly no. The moment she said "lead" I was already in. I've been rotting at home for three weeks with nothing to run, nothing to decide. My mom keeps asking why I don't just relax like a normal person during break. But sitting around feels like drowning. At least now I have something with my name on it.

I'm so frustrated I could scream. The orientation committee met today on Zoom and Ms. Liu, who's been at the school for fifteen years, kept interrupting me to "add context" every time I proposed something. Context! As if I don't know our own students. She literally said "Wei-Lin, you're still new here" in front of everyone when I suggested we move the welcome assembly outdoors to the courtyard.

It's my committee. Principal Chen asked ME to lead it. But every time I try to actually lead, someone older swoops in to remind me I'm twenty-three. I left banking so I wouldn't have to wait in line behind people who got there first. I thought teaching would be different.

Nudge: "Different how?"

Response: I thought... I don't know. That being good at something would be enough. At the bank I was fast, accurate, never made mistakes, but promotions went by seniority. I figured teaching would reward initiative—you put in the work, you earn the room. But maybe every institution is the same. Maybe I'm just chasing something that doesn't exist, some place where being capable matters more than being old. That's a depressing thought. I don't want to believe it.

Same day, different mood I guess. After that Zoom call and venting here I went for a long walk around the harbor, watched the fishing boats come in, and something clicked. Ms. Liu wasn't trying to undermine me—she's been running orientations since before I was in middle school myself, and she probably felt sidelined when Chen handed the lead role to someone who's been there less than two years.

That doesn't mean I was wrong to want to shake things up. The outdoor assembly IS a better idea. But maybe I came in too hot, treating the meeting like a presentation where I had all the answers instead of a collaboration where people needed to feel heard. My dad used to say I sprint before people have tied their shoes. He meant it as a flaw. I always took it as a compliment.

I think both can be true? I move fast because slow feels unbearable, but fast doesn't have to mean alone. I messaged Ms. Liu after the walk and asked if she wanted to grab boba tomorrow to talk through the logistics together. She sent back a smiley face. Small win. I'll take it.

The harbor smelled like diesel and salt and I took some good shots of the cranes against the sunset for the gram. Feeling more like myself now. Sometimes I just need to move to think.

Spent the whole afternoon with Abu Mahmoud at his farm outside Karameh. Three hours sitting on plastic chairs drinking tea while he showed me where the irrigation canal used to run before the allocation changed. His hands, the way he traced the old waterline in the dirt like drawing a map from memory. Forty years he worked that land. Now his grandson is driving trucks in Amman because there is nothing left to grow.

I recorded everything but the recording will not capture how quiet he got when I asked about the cooperative's petition. How he looked past me toward the orchards, half of them dried stumps now. Dr. Khalil wants regression models and policy impact matrices. What I have is Abu Mahmoud saying "we wrote letters until we ran out of paper." This is what I came here for. Not the degree, not the credentials. This.

Nudge: "What made you leave engineering for this?"

Response: Thirty years I designed systems to move water from where it was to where it was needed. Good work. Useful work. But always I was working for someone else's priorities, someone else's calculations about who matters. The last project, we diverted a whole tributary for a development that displaced six villages. I signed my name to those drawings. Came home and could not sleep for a week. Told my husband I needed to understand the other side of my own blueprints.

Committee meeting today. Dr. Khalil brought up timeline again, asked when I would have my methodology chapter finalized. I told him the fieldwork is taking longer because I am doing it properly, not rushing through interviews like they are survey forms. He gave me that look, the one he gives when he thinks I am being difficult. Said something about how at my stage of career I should know how to work within constraints.

At my stage. Meaning old. Meaning I should be grateful they let me in at all, should play by the rules without questioning. He does not say this directly but I hear it. The younger students in my cohort, they nod along, they revise when told to revise. Maybe that is smarter. Maybe I am wasting everyone's time insisting my approach matters. But every time I sit down to write the chapter his way, my hands just stop. Something in me refuses.

Nudge: "What specifically about his way makes your hands stop?"

Response: He wants me to treat the farmers as data points. Sample size, response rates, coding categories. I understand the logic, I am not stupid about methodology. But when I sit down to write it that way, I keep seeing Abu Mahmoud's face. He trusted me with his story. Reducing it to a variable in a regression feels like betraying that trust. Like I am doing the same thing I did as an engineer, just with different paperwork.

Roommates had people over until 2am again. Studied at the library until they kicked me out, then sat in my car in the parking lot reading by phone light. Called Farshad and he laughed, said I am the most stubborn person he knows.

Nudge: "What were you reading in the car?"

Response: Papers on participatory action research. Found this scholar from Brazil who worked with fishing communities fighting industrial pollution. Her whole methodology is about the community being partners in the research, not subjects. Reading her citations like following a trail. If Dr. Khalil will not accept my approach, maybe I can build enough of a foundation that he cannot dismiss it as naive.

Presented the participatory framework to the committee today. Dr. Khalil called it unconventional. Dr. Haddad said she found it compelling. Got approval to continue with modifications. Walked out of that room feeling like I had earned something that was mine.

First real day of summer break. Slept until 10, made a proper French press coffee, and read on the porch for two hours. This is why I teach.

Nudge: "What do you mean when you say this is why you teach?"

Response: Honestly, I just meant the break is nice. Not everything needs to be deep. The coffee was good, the weather was perfect, and I didn't have to think about lesson plans. That's enough.

Spent the week at a cabin in Wisconsin with Jenna. No cell service for five days. Best decision we've made in months.

Nudge: "What made the no-cell-service part particularly good for you?"

Got back from Wisconsin and immediately checked my email. Mistake. There's already an email from the department head about "optional" summer curriculum planning sessions. Optional in quotes because we all know how that goes. Last year three of us skipped the sessions and somehow ended up with the worst schedules in the fall.

I'm not doing it this year. I told Jenna I'm ignoring the email until August 1st at the earliest, and she actually applauded. Twelve years in and I've finally learned that the school will survive without me reworking the sophomore reading list in July. The people who show up to these things aren't more dedicated than me - they just haven't figured out how to protect their own time yet.

Made paella from scratch today. Took four hours and cost more than ordering it from La Taberna, but there's something about the process that makes it worth it. Jenna said it was the best meal I've made this summer.

Nudge: "What is it about the process that makes it worth the extra time and money?"

Response: It's the ritual of it, I think. Standing over the stove, adjusting the heat, watching the rice absorb the broth. My brain actually turns off when I'm cooking something complicated. No thinking about September syllabi or parent emails. Just saffron and timing. The four hours weren't a cost - they were the point.

Today was my parents' 40th wedding anniversary, and I spent most of the afternoon at their villa helping coordinate everything. My mother had very specific ideas about the flower arrangements and the seating chart — cousin Layla absolutely cannot be seated near aunt Fatima after what happened at Eid — and I found myself nodding along, rearranging the entire dining room twice to meet her vision.

The funny thing is, I didn't mind. There's something calming about fitting into the role everyone expects. My father pulled me aside after the guests arrived and thanked me for "being the reliable one," and I felt this warmth in my chest. My younger brother Samir flew in from London but spent most of the party on his phone, and I could see the disappointment in my mother's eyes. I don't want to be the source of that look.

But there was this moment, late in the evening, when the older relatives were doing their formal toasts, and I looked across the room at my wife Nadia. She caught my eye and did this little mock-yawn behind her napkin, and I had to bite my cheek not to laugh. We'd both rather have been at that new seafood place on the Palm with a bottle of wine, just the two of us. Instead, I sat through two hours of my uncle's stories about the old days.

I think I'm okay with it. The party was beautiful. Everyone was happy. And Nadia and I are going to that restaurant next weekend anyway — I already made the reservation. You can do both, I think. Be the person your family needs and still carve out space for the things that make life sweet.

I'm so frustrated I could scream. We had our quarterly review today and Hamza — my direct report who I've mentored for two years — threw me under the bus in front of the regional director. He presented his supply chain optimization idea as if it was entirely his own, when we literally developed it together over coffee last month. I was the one who told him to run the numbers on the Oman route!

The worst part is I just sat there. Smiled. Nodded along. When Ahmed, my boss, looked at me after the presentation, I said something supportive about Hamza's initiative. What else was I supposed to do? Create a scene? Interrupt him mid-presentation to claim credit? That's not how things are done. You don't embarrass a colleague publicly, especially one you outrank. It would look petty. Undignified.

But God, I wanted to. I wanted to say, "Actually, that was our idea, developed during our Tuesday coffees." Instead I shook his hand after and told him he did well. He had the grace to look slightly uncomfortable, at least.

Nadia says I should have spoken up privately to Ahmed afterward. She's probably right. But I keep thinking about how my father handled workplace conflicts — with patience, with letting things play out, with trusting that people eventually see the truth. Maybe Hamza will acknowledge it later. Maybe Ahmed already knows whose brain that idea came from.

Or maybe I'm just telling myself stories to feel better about being a coward. I don't know. I do know that I came home and ate an entire box of those pistachio sweets from that place in Sharjah, and I don't regret it one bit. Sometimes you just need something good after a bad day.

Nudge: "You mentioned Nadia's suggestion and your father's approach as two different paths. Which one feels more true to who you want to be?"

Response: Honestly? I think I'm overthinking this. It's one meeting. Hamza is young, he got excited about presenting something that worked. The idea will get implemented either way, and that's what matters for the team. I'd rather focus on the fact that we're on track for a really good Q4, and I'm taking Nadia to Bahrain for New Year's. Some things just aren't worth the energy of picking apart.

Can't sleep. It's 2am and I'm sitting on the balcony with my laptop because Nadia's breathing is usually soothing but tonight it's just reminding me that everyone else can rest except me.

My mother called today and mentioned — casually, as if she was discussing the weather — that cousin Layla's husband just got made partner at his firm and isn't that wonderful, they're buying a bigger house in Abu Dhabi. Then she asked when Nadia and I are going to "settle down properly" and have children. I'm 41. We've been married six years. She knows we've been trying. She knows about the appointments with Dr. Khalil. But she still asks, every single time, like maybe if she reminds me enough, my body will cooperate.

I didn't say anything. I made the appropriate congratulatory noises about Layla's husband. I changed the subject to the marina and the boat engine I'm having fixed.

What I wanted to say was: we might not be able to have children, and it's breaking Nadia apart, and I feel like a failure every time she cries in the bathroom thinking I can't hear her. But you don't burden your mother with things like that. You don't make her worry. You don't disrupt the pleasant fiction that everything is fine and her eldest son has everything figured out.

The thing is, I don't even know if I want children anymore. Is that terrible? I used to. I had the whole picture in my head — teaching my son to fish, taking my daughter to her first day of school. But lately I've been thinking about what our life looks like without them. The freedom. The travel. The late Sunday mornings. Is that selfish? It feels selfish. But it also feels honest.

I should go back to bed. Nadia's got that look lately when I stay up too late, like she's worried about me. I don't want to give her another thing to worry about.

Nudge: "You described the picture you used to have and the one you're starting to imagine now. What would it take to talk about this with Nadia?"

Response: That's the thing — I'm not sure I can. We've never actually said out loud that it might not happen. We talk around it. We talk about appointments, and procedures, and "next steps." But we don't talk about what it means if there are no next steps. I think I'm afraid that if I tell her I'm starting to be okay with the idea of just us, she'll think I've given up on her. On us. On the future she wanted. And I haven't given up. I'm just... adapting? Protecting myself maybe? I don't know. I think I need to figure out what I actually feel before I bring it to her. Right now it's all just noise in my head at 2am.

Bahrain trip was good. Nadia and I finally talked about the fertility stuff. She cried but also seemed sort of relieved, I think? We're going to take a break from the treatments for a few months.

Nudge: "That sounds like a meaningful conversation to have had together. How are you feeling now that it's out in the open?"

Response: You know, mostly I'm just glad to be back at work tomorrow. The holiday period always feels long. We have the Q1 planning meeting on Thursday and I want to make sure the Oman route data is properly credited this time. Fresh start to the year.

Sat through the whole lab meeting biting my tongue while Felix presented my preprocessing idea as his own. Didn't say anything. Walked home in the rain and honestly the cold felt better than sitting in that room. At least I know the idea works — that's mine regardless of who gets credit.

Nudge: "What stopped you from speaking up?"

People keep asking why I turned down Professor Hecht's placement like I owe them an explanation. It was a bad fit. I don't need to justify building something from scratch when the alternative is tweaking someone else's framework for two years. My project, my call.

Nudge: "You sound sure, but you haven't told your family yet."

Response: Yeah, because telling them means a two-hour phone call where my mother explains how Professor Hecht's name on my CV would open doors. She's not wrong about that part. I just can't spend two years on work that doesn't feel like mine. I'll tell them once I have something to show for it — results shut down the lecture faster than arguments do.

Three days into building my own pipeline and I'm already behind on coursework. Stayed up until four last night debugging tokenizer issues that would've been solved if I'd just used the lab's existing toolkit. My officemate Nina asked if I wanted help and I said no before I even thought about it. That was stupid. She's good at this stuff and she wasn't going to steal the project.

I keep doing this — refusing help because I need it to be entirely mine, and then drowning quietly. The fellowship application is due Friday and I haven't started the personal statement because every time I open it I think about how I'm supposed to frame myself as a team player. I don't know how to write that without it feeling like a lie, but I also don't know how to explain that I work better alone without sounding like a problem.

Had a meeting with the regional leads this morning and Marcus just talked right over me when I was laying out the Q2 headcount plan. I was mid-sentence — literally had the slide up — and he just started redirecting the conversation toward his own hiring timeline. Nobody stopped him. Janet, who was running the meeting, just went with it and moved on.

I sat there for the rest of the hour barely saying anything. I had the data. I had prepared for this since Thursday. And it just got swallowed up because Marcus is louder and has been in the room longer. The worst part is I could feel myself doing the thing where I just go quiet instead of pushing back, and I hated it. My chest was tight and I kept rehearsing what I should have said.

Afterwards, one of the other leads came up to me and said "good points earlier" which somehow made it worse. Like, you heard me but you didn't back me up when it counted? I don't know what to do with that.

Nudge: "What stopped you from cutting back in?"

Response: Honestly? The thought that if I pushed it and Marcus pushed back harder, I'd look like the one making a scene. There's this calculation I do instantly — will I come out of this looking stronger or weaker — and in that moment the math didn't feel right. Which I realize sounds insane because saying nothing definitely made me look weaker. I think I'm more afraid of losing ground publicly than I am of just being invisible.

Submitted the facilities request for the new storage bay expansion three weeks ago. Need sign-off from Dave in operations finance before anything moves. Followed up twice, got a "should have something for you by end of week" both times. Third week running.

Meanwhile my team is stacking pallets in the overflow lot and we're burning hours on workarounds that wouldn't exist if someone would just approve the spend. I called Dave's office again at four o'clock, left a voicemail. Sat at my desk staring at the phone for twenty minutes afterward like it was going to ring back.

Nudge: "Is it the wait, or that you have to wait?"

Response: The second one. The delay is annoying but it's the dependency that actually gets to me. I've run the numbers, I know the ROI, and none of that matters because it's Dave's signature on the line. My whole team's efficiency is bottlenecked by someone who probably hasn't even opened the attachment.

Karen from procurement shot down my vendor swap proposal in front of the entire ops meeting. I had solid margins data and she just kept saying "that's not how we onboard vendors here." I folded. Just said fine, we'll keep the current contract, because I could tell nobody else was going to back me up and I wasn't going to beg.

Quiet Saturday. Took Logan to his baseball game, they won 6-3. He made a nice catch in the third inning and looked over at me in the dugout immediately. That part was good.

Spent some time after the game reorganizing the garage because the shelf brackets have been loose for weeks and it was bothering me. Put on a podcast about supply chain trends, half listened. Thought about whether I should bring the vendor swap thing back up on Monday or just let it die. Karen's probably already forgotten about it but I haven't.

Did some meal prep for the week. Texted my brother about mom's birthday dinner logistics — he still hasn't confirmed how many people he's bringing, which is exactly the kind of thing that drives me nuts. Just tell me a number so I can book the table.

Nudge: "What would letting the vendor thing go feel like?"

Response: It's not really about feelings, it's about the fact that we're paying twelve percent more than we need to. Anyway, I'll probably just bring it up again differently. Frame it as a cost reduction initiative instead of a vendor change. Same thing, different packaging.

Had my quarterly review with Janet. She flagged my team's throughput numbers — second highest in the region, up four percent from last quarter. She said it straight, no fluff, and I appreciated that. Then she asked if I'd be willing to present the process improvements at the May leadership summit.

I said yes immediately. Didn't even hesitate. There's a version of me from five years ago who would've been nervous about standing in front of that room, but right now I just want them to see what my team has built. We earned this. I drove home with the windows down and felt genuinely good for the first time in a while.

Nudge: "What's different about you now versus five years ago?"

Response: I mean, five years ago I was still an analyst hoping someone would notice my work. Now I have a team, I have numbers, I have a track record. It's not some deep transformation — I just have receipts now.

New guy on the team, Derek, has been here two months. In the standup today he started explaining the pallet tracking system to me — the one I designed three years ago. He was walking me through it like I was the new hire. I let him finish because I didn't want to embarrass him in front of everyone, but it sat with me all morning.

Later, one of my senior leads, Tanya, mentioned Derek had been asking her questions about "how things work around here" instead of coming to me. Not sure if he just doesn't know the hierarchy or if he's intentionally going around me. Either way, it doesn't feel great.

Ate the last of the good cheese after lunch today, the one from the market on Saturday. Just sat at the table with it and a coffee and some crackers. Then spent the rest of the afternoon annoyed at myself for not going straight back to the loom, as if ten minutes of sitting with cheese somehow cancelled out the six hours I'd already put in.

Finished the second panel for the Kungsbacka commission today. When I stepped back and looked at it hung up on the temporary rail, I actually liked it. Not in the way where you talk yourself into liking something because you spent forty hours on it. I genuinely liked the way the indigo section catches the light from the side window. I stood there for a while just looking.

Then I let myself leave the studio at four instead of pushing through to six. Walked down to the harbor and sat on the bench near the ferry terminal. Bought a cardamom bun from the kiosk. It was still warm. The whole walk home I felt good, and for once I didn't ruin it by calculating whether I'd done enough that day to justify leaving early. I just walked.

My friend Karin came over tonight and we cooked together. She brought wine and we made a ridiculous amount of pasta and talked about nothing important for three hours. I didn't think about the commission once, which surprises me even writing it now.

Nudge: "What was different about tonight?"

Teaching day. The folk school wanted me to stay for the staff meeting after my afternoon class, so I did. It ran an hour longer than it needed to because people kept going in circles about the spring exhibition. I had opinions but didn't push them because I'm only there two days a week and it's not my place to take over other people's planning.

Someone asked what I'm working on in my own studio and I gave a short answer. I don't like talking about commissioned work with the folk school crowd because it always turns into a conversation about whether commercial work counts as real art. It does, for the record. The Kungsbacka library is going to have my panels on their walls for the next twenty years. But I don't feel like defending that every time.

Long day but a strangely satisfying one. Woke up early because the light was doing that thing it does in November here, grey but somehow bright at the same time, and I wanted to see how it would fall across the third panel. So I was in the studio by seven, still in my wool socks, coffee going cold on the shelf behind me because I forgot about it.

Worked through until about one without stopping. Not because I was forcing myself but because the work was pulling me along. The warp tension on the new section was exactly right and the shuttle was moving the way I wanted. Those stretches don't happen every day and when they do you just stay in them.

After I stopped I heated up leftover soup and ate it standing at the kitchen counter, which I know isn't a proper lunch but it was warm and salty and I was hungry enough that it tasted better than it had any right to. Then I lay down on the sofa for twenty minutes. Just lay there with the radio on low. Didn't set a timer, didn't think about what I should be doing instead. I was tired enough that my body made the decision for me and I didn't argue with it. The afternoon I spent doing the boring parts, measuring and marking the next section, but even that felt okay because the morning had been so good.

Writing again because something small happened after I put this down earlier. I was brushing my teeth before bed and noticed I'd left the studio light on, so I went in to switch it off. Ended up standing there in the dark with just the hallway light coming through the door, looking at the panels. They looked different at night. Quieter.

I almost went back to the loom. There was that pull, that voice that says you could get another hour in. But I didn't. I turned the light off and closed the door. It felt like a small right thing, choosing to stop. Going to bed now.

Nudge: "What made stopping feel right tonight?"

Response: Because the day had already been full. I didn't owe it anything else.

Had a one-on-one with my manager today where he brought up the new "technical design review board" they're implementing. Starting in January, any architectural change needs to go through a committee of three senior engineers before we can even start coding. I nodded along and said it made sense for quality control, but inside I was thinking about how this is the third approval layer they've added this year.

Part of me gets it. We've had incidents. There's regulatory pressure. The team has grown from 12 to 45 people and you can't have everyone doing their own thing. But I miss the days when I could just have an idea, prototype it over a weekend, and ship it by Wednesday if it worked.

Spent the entire morning in back-to-back meetings about the new microservice architecture. Everyone has opinions, nobody wants to make a decision, and we ended up exactly where we started. I finally just said "look, why don't I build a proof of concept this week and we can evaluate from there?" My tech lead looked uncomfortable and reminded me we need to follow the new process.

The thing is, I know the process exists for good reasons. Last year Tomas pushed that caching change without proper review and it took down production for three hours. But there's got to be a middle ground between chaos and paralysis. Came home irritated and Maja asked what was wrong. I said "nothing, just work stuff" because I didn't want to vent about the same complaints again.

Nudge: "You mentioned not wanting to vent to Maja again. How often do you find yourself holding back like that?"

Response: Probably more than I should. It's just that she's heard the same story before - Erik is frustrated at work, Erik thinks things should be different. At some point it starts to feel self-indulgent. She has her own stress with the kids' schedules and her job. I don't want to be the guy who comes home every day complaining about meetings and processes. So I just... keep it to myself, put the kids to bed, then tinker with my side projects until midnight. It's fine. It works.

Sunday dinner at Maja's parents' place. Her father asked me about work and I gave the usual "it's going well, keeping busy" response. What I didn't mention was that I've been quietly looking at job postings this week. Nothing serious, just... looking. There's a startup in Gothenburg that's hiring senior engineers and their tech blog posts suggest they actually let people build things without eighteen layers of review.

But then I think about what leaving would mean. We bought this apartment two years ago specifically because it's close to Maja's parents and the kids' school. My in-laws help with pickup three days a week. The kids have their friends, their routines. And honestly, the current job pays well, has good benefits, and the people are mostly nice. It's just the bureaucracy that gets to me.

Over dinner, my father-in-law started talking about how his generation valued loyalty to employers and how job-hopping is "a young person's disease." I just smiled and nodded and said something about stability being important. Maja caught my eye across the table and I could tell she knew something was off, but we didn't talk about it in the car ride home. The kids were tired and there wasn't space for that conversation.

Nudge: "You smiled and agreed with your father-in-law about loyalty, while quietly browsing job postings. What's it like holding both of those at once?"

Response: Uncomfortable, if I'm being honest. I'm not sure if I was lying to him or to myself. Part of me does believe in loyalty - I've given this company seven years and I've built things I'm proud of. But another part of me wonders if loyalty is just another word for being afraid to rock the boat.

The thing is, I don't actually know if I'd be happier somewhere else, or if I'd just find new things to be frustrated about. Maybe the startup in Gothenburg would turn into the same bureaucratic mess in three years. Maybe the problem is that I want to do things my way and the world doesn't work like that when you're part of a team. I haven't figured that out yet.

Submitted my first technical design document to the new review board today. It took me two hours to write up something I could have just built in four. Three diagrams, a risk assessment matrix, rollback procedures. The whole thing. And you know what? They approved it in the meeting with almost no changes. The tech lead even said it was "exactly the kind of thorough documentation we need."

I should feel vindicated but mostly I just feel tired. I did the thing. I followed the process. And now I get to actually code, which is what I wanted to do a week ago. Maybe this is just what being a senior engineer at a growing company looks like. You learn to work within the system instead of against it. On the way home I stopped to pick up the kids from football practice and my daughter asked if I could help her build a website for her school project. I said yes immediately. At least that's one thing I can still build without a committee.

Had to let Fadi go today. Three years we built this together, back when it was just the two of us sharing a desk in my cousin's spare room. But he kept going around me to the team, kept second-guessing my calls in front of everyone. The look on his face when I handed him the paperwork, like I had betrayed something sacred between us.

Maybe I did. I keep thinking about what my grandmother used to say about loyalty, about how a man is nothing without the people who stood by him when he had nothing. Fadi stood by me. He believed in me before anyone else did. But a company cannot have two captains, and every time he questioned me publicly, I could feel the team's respect slipping away, feel my grip loosening.

I called Amira after, hoping she would understand. She just went quiet, then said something about how I seem different lately, harder. I told her running a business requires hard decisions. She said there is a difference between hard and cold. I hung up first, which I never do. Now it is 2am and I am staring at the ceiling wondering if she is right, if I am becoming someone even I would not recognize.

Nudge: "What exactly did Fadi say that made you feel your grip loosening?"

Response: Last month he told Rami, our lead developer, that my timeline for the Saudi expansion was unrealistic, and that maybe we should slow down. Did not tell me first. I heard it from Rami, who looked at me differently after that. Like maybe Fadi was the reasonable one and I was just chasing something too fast. That moment, I knew I had already lost part of the team to him, even if they would never say it out loud.

The investor meeting did not go how I imagined. Omar Khalifeh, the one everyone said would write the check without blinking, spent the whole time asking about our advisory board, our governance structure, whether we had brought on a more experienced CEO. I told him I am the CEO. He smiled like I had said something charming.

I used to think the money would just follow if the numbers were good enough. Our numbers are excellent. But he kept looking at me like I was a kid playing at something serious. I mentioned Fadi leaving and he actually said that was concerning, that investors like to see stable founding teams. Walked out of there feeling smaller than I have in months. Started drafting a message to Fadi, then deleted it.

Nudge: "What were you going to say to Fadi before you deleted it?"

Woke up at 4am again. Instead of spiraling about Omar or Fadi or any of it, I went to the office early and just sat in the dark for a while. Watched the sun come up through the glass. Something about being there before anyone else, the whole floor quiet and mine.

Rami came in around seven, surprised to see me. We ended up talking for two hours about the product roadmap, just the two of us hashing things out like the old days. No posturing, no defending my position. He had ideas I would have dismissed a month ago, but today I actually listened. Some of them were good. Told him so. His whole face changed, like he had been waiting for permission to think out loud.

Strange how that felt more like control than anything I have done in weeks. Not control over him, but control over myself. Over the kind of leader I am choosing to be. Amira called in the afternoon and I told her about the conversation with Rami. She said it sounded like I was finding my way back to something. I think she might be right. Not softer, not weaker. Just more certain about what actually matters.

Ran the thesis committee meeting today and something just clicked into place. Dr. Farouk actually deferred to me when the methodology question came up, turned to me in front of the whole room and said "Nasser has more field experience here." I didn't even have to push for it. The newer students were taking notes when I spoke about the interview protocol design and one of them asked afterward if I could review her proposal. I said yes of course.

There's a particular feeling when the room orients toward you not because you demanded it but because they recognize you belong at the front. I've spent months building that credibility here — showing up early, preparing sharper questions than anyone else, making sure my comments in seminars land with weight. It's not about ego. It's about earning the position through the work.

Walking back to my office I passed the department bulletin board where they posted the council restructuring I proposed last semester. My name on the memo. My framework. Small thing maybe, but I stood there for a minute just reading it. Some days the years in government feel like lost time, like I should have done the PhD at twenty-five. But today it felt like those years are exactly what make me the person the room turns to.

So apparently Huda told Dr. Kassar that my proposed panel structure for the symposium was "unnecessarily hierarchical." Her words got back to me through Tariq. I set it up that way for a reason — someone has to manage the flow, assign speaking order, handle Q&A. That's not hierarchy, that's organization. If she wanted a different format she could have brought it up in the planning meeting instead of going behind my back.

What bothers me is not the criticism, it's the route she took. She didn't come to me. She went to the professor. Like I'm someone you go around rather than talk to. I've been running events longer than she's been in the program and I know what works. If the panel falls apart without structure, nobody's going to blame Huda for it.

Nudge: "You keep saying it's about the route she took — but what if it's actually the word 'hierarchical' that got to you?"

Response: It's really not about the word. I just don't appreciate back-channel conversations.

Had an uncomfortable exchange with my supervisor today that I keep replaying. Dr. Kassar suggested I "consider co-authoring" the working paper with Huda and Youssef instead of submitting it solo. He framed it as a "collaborative opportunity" but it felt like a demotion. I've done ninety percent of the literature review, the entire theoretical framework is mine, and now I'm supposed to share the byline because it builds "departmental cohesion."

I didn't argue in the moment. I nodded and said I'd think about it. But my chest was tight the whole walk home and I can still feel it now sitting at my desk. My name on that paper matters. It's my ticket to the next conference, maybe a publication that separates my CV from every other ABD candidate. Sharing credit when I did the bulk of the work is not collaboration, it's redistribution.

The worst part is I know saying no will look petty. I'll be the guy who wouldn't share, the one who doesn't play well with others. But saying yes means my contribution gets diluted. It means standing on a stage and introducing a paper that used to be mine as "ours." I keep drafting the email in my head and both versions — yes and no — make me feel sick.

Nudge: "What exactly changes about the paper if two other names are on it?"

Response: It stops being proof that I can produce something significant on my own. The paper is the same but the signal it sends is completely different — it becomes a group project instead of evidence that I belong at the front of a room making an argument nobody else in this department could make.

Still thinking about the co-authorship thing, hours later now. I called my brother Faisal and he said I was overthinking it, that in his engineering firm people share credit on projects all the time and nobody cares. Easy for him to say — he's a senior manager with his name on the letterhead already. He doesn't need a single paper to prove anything. I tried to explain that in academia authorship order matters, that first-author versus third-author is a different career, but I could hear him losing interest.

What really got under my skin was when he said "not everything has to have your stamp on it, Nasser." He meant it gently. I know that. But it landed like an accusation. Like there's something wrong with wanting recognition for what I built. My father spent thirty years in the civil service and retired with a ceremony and a clock. Nobody remembered his name six months later. I watched that happen and I decided early on that I would not let my work disappear into an institution's memory.

Maybe Faisal's right that I'm overthinking. But the feeling hasn't gone away. I keep imagining the conference program with three names where mine used to be alone, and it physically bothers me. Not rage, not sadness exactly. More like watching someone move your chair at a table you set.

Good class today. The students actually debated about the new coastal development project instead of just waiting for me to give them the "right" answer. These moments remind me why I chose this path.

Nudge: "What made them engage differently today?"

Response: I think they're just more comfortable with the format now. We've been doing discussions for a few weeks.

Long day. Parent-teacher meetings are draining in a way that teaching never is. One parent spent twenty minutes explaining why her son shouldn't have to learn about "foreign problems" like ocean pollution. I tried to explain that the Arabian Sea is not foreign to Kerala, but I could see her eyes glazing over.

Then the principal pulled me aside afterward to suggest I "tone down the activism" in my lessons. Said some parents have been complaining. I nodded and said I'd think about it. Not sure what I'm supposed to think about, honestly.

Nudge: "What did that nod cost you?"

Response: Nothing. It's just politics. Part of working in any institution.

Spent the weekend thinking about the principal's comment. I talked it over with Arjun and he made a good point - I can either fight every battle loudly and burn out, or I can be strategic. Pick what matters most and find ways to teach it that don't raise red flags.

I don't love the idea of softening my approach. But I also know that if I get pushed out, whoever replaces me will probably just teach straight from the textbook. At least I'm here. At least these kids are asking questions about their own coastline. That has to count for something.
